---
title: "wcEcoli, briefly: what a whole-cell model actually is"
description: "Simulating every known molecule in a cell over time — what that means, and what it doesn't."
author: "Steve Haigh"
date: "2026-05-27"
categories: [research, modelling]
draft: true
---

*[Scaffold — fill in.]*

A whole-cell model is what it sounds like: a simulation that includes every annotated molecular component of a single cell — genome, transcriptome, proteome, metabolism, regulation — and integrates them across one division cycle. The Covert lab's *wcEcoli* is the canonical implementation, built around *E. coli* MG1655.

## What it includes

*[Components, scale, time-stepping. ~30k reactions, the metabolite/protein/gene layers, the cell-division integration. Stochastic where appropriate; deterministic ODE for metabolism.]*

## What it doesn't

*[Not every molecule — only what's annotated. Skips spatial structure. Doesn't predict novel mechanisms; encodes existing knowledge.]*

## What it's good for

*[Hypothesis generation; in silico knockouts; coverage gaps in the literature; sanity-checking proposed mechanisms; finding places where the literature is internally inconsistent.]*

## What it isn't a replacement for

*[Wet lab. The model encodes what we already know.]*

## Why I'm extending it to platelets

*[Briefly: the platelet is a tractable cell to model — small genome of pre-loaded mRNA, no transcription, clear inputs and outputs. wcEcoli's framework adapts. Link to the platelet post when written.]*
